title: The I-5 Strangler: Roger Kibbe's Long Trail 📁
segments: Between 1977 and 1988, women began disappearing from rest stops, gas stations, and roadsides along Interstate 5 in Northern California. Their bodies were found in remote canyon roads, wooded turnouts, and ravines. Some were strangled. Others were stabbed. The pattern took investigators more than a decade to fully understand.

The first confirmed victim of the man later known as the I-5 Strangler was Lou Ellen Burleigh, 21. She vanished from Walnut Creek in September 1977 after meeting a stranger about a possible secretarial job. Her body has never been found.

Through the late 1970s and 1980s, women in their teens and twenties were lured by what investigators believe were similar tactics — a man offering rides, asking about job ads, or appearing helpful at a roadside stop.
Several victims were found nude or partially clothed, with their clothing cut in distinctive patterns. The cuts were precise. They formed a fingerprint of sorts.

On July 3, 1986, Charmaine Sabrah disappeared from her broken-down car on I-5 near Sacramento. Her mother had stayed with the vehicle. A man stopped, offered Charmaine a ride to find help, and drove off with her. Her body was found weeks later.
On April 20, 1987, Lora Heedick was last seen at a Mountain View bar. Her body was discovered along Highway 12. The same precise cuts on her clothing matched earlier victims.

Sacramento County investigators began to formally connect the cases under the working name 'I-5 Strangler.' The pattern stretched from the Bay Area up through Sacramento and toward the Sierra foothills.

Here's where investigators caught a break. In September 1987, a woman named Debra Ann Guffie was attacked in a Sacramento parking lot by a man who handcuffed her, gagged her, and dragged her toward a vehicle. Police responding to her screams arrested the man on the spot.
His name was Roger Reece Kibbe. He was 48, a Sacramento resident, and a furniture-business owner. Investigators found rope, a knife, scissors, and a roll of duct tape inside his car.

When detectives searched Kibbe's home, they found women's clothing with the same distinctive cuts seen on the I-5 victims. Investigators connected the physical evidence to at least one murder almost immediately.

Kibbe was charged with the 1986 murder of Darcie Frackenpohl, a 17-year-old runaway whose body had been found in Eldorado County. The DNA technology of the time was limited. The clothing-cut signature was the lead piece of evidence.
In 1991, Kibbe was convicted of Frackenpohl's murder. He received a 25-years-to-life sentence. Investigators believed he was responsible for several more killings, but evidence was thin.

Then came one of the most patient cold-case investigations in California history. Detective Vito Bertocchini and a team of cold-case investigators sat with Kibbe in the early 2000s, sharing case files, building rapport, looking for openings.

In 2009, after years of off-and-on conversations, Kibbe agreed to plead guilty to six additional murders in exchange for life without parole. He confessed to the killings of Sabrah, Heedick, Burleigh, Stephanie Brown, Katherine Quinones, and Barbara Ann Scott.
Each confession unlocked details only the killer could have known. Sabrah's last words. The location of items removed from victims. The reasons certain women were chosen.

Kibbe described himself as compulsive. He told investigators the cutting of clothing was tied to a fetish that had escalated for years before the killings began. He was unable to fully explain when or why he stopped.
California authorities credit the cold-case team's slow approach. Bertocchini once said in a 2015 interview that Kibbe responded to patience the way other suspects responded to pressure.

On February 28, 2021, Roger Kibbe was strangled inside his cell at Mule Creek State Prison by his cellmate, Jason Budrow, a convicted murderer. Budrow later told investigators that Kibbe had bragged about uncharged crimes and that he killed him as a kind of punishment.
Budrow was charged with murder. Kibbe's death closed the prosecutorial chapter but reopened questions about how many more victims there might be.

Investigators in California, Oregon, and Washington have reviewed unsolved roadside murders dating to the late 1960s and early 1970s. Kibbe lived in San Diego before moving north and could have begun killing earlier than the Burleigh case.

The Lou Ellen Burleigh disappearance remains technically unsolved in terms of remains. Her family has continued to push for a search, but vast areas of remote California foothills make recovery unlikely.

What investigators have learned from Kibbe is now part of FBI behavioral analysis training. His clothing-cut signature, his selection of broken-down vehicles and rest stops, and his decade-long arc of escalation are studied at the Behavioral Analysis Unit in Quantico.

Several documentary projects have profiled the case, including detailed work by California reporters who covered Kibbe's plea hearings in 2009. The transcripts of those proceedings are publicly available through Sacramento Superior Court.

If you knew anyone who disappeared in California, Oregon, or Washington along Interstate 5 between roughly 1968 and 1988, you can contact the California Department of Justice cold case unit. The Kibbe file is open for additional victim identification.
